pattern: It is far easier to count the numbers whose digits are all distinct and subtract from n, using combinatorics over digit positions to count distinct-digit numbers below the bound.
hints: Counting numbers with a repeated digit directly is awkward; count the complement instead — numbers in the range with all distinct digits — and subtract from n.
Numbers with fewer digits than n are easy: for a d-digit number the first digit has nine choices and each later digit one fewer remaining choice, so multiply those falling factorials.

For numbers with exactly as many digits as n, fix each prefix digit equal to n's digits and count the distinct-digit completions for smaller choices at each position, stopping early if n itself repeats a digit.

edge_inputs.description: exactly one repeated-digit number up to twenty
bound with a repeat in the middle

stress.description: random bounds small enough for the brute-force oracle to enumerate
stress.note: kept small so the digit-by-digit brute force finishes well under the timeout
